author: Author of 2 Clement
interpretation: The author of 2 Clement echoes 2 Peter's concern about the delayed return of Christ. He urges believers not to interpret the delay as evidence that the promise has failed but as an opportunity for repentance. The day of judgment will come, and when it does, 'the whole earth shall be a molten mass of fire.' Believers must live in holiness and expectation, not complacency.
context: 2 Clement (actually an early Christian homily, not by Clement) shows that the problem of the delayed parousia was a live pastoral concern in the mid-second century. Its exhortation closely parallels 2 Peter's, suggesting the two texts address the same cultural moment of waning eschatological expectation.

author: Origen of Alexandria
interpretation: Origen reads the cosmic conflagration in verse 10 allegorically: the 'elements' that will be destroyed by fire are not physical matter but the evil spiritual powers that corrupt creation. The 'new heavens and new earth' (v. 13) represent the renewed spiritual order after God purifies creation of all evil. Origen's broader eschatology of universal restoration (apokatastasis) means the fire is ultimately purifying, not annihilating — it burns away sin to liberate creation.
context: Origen's spiritualizing reading was characteristic of the Alexandrian school and stands in sharp contrast to the literal cosmic catastrophe that most ancient readers expected. His allegorical approach was influential but not dominant; the literal expectation of cosmic renewal remained the majority view.

author: Augustine of Hippo
interpretation: Augustine reads the passage literally: the present world will be destroyed by fire and replaced by a new creation. The 'one day is like a thousand years' (v. 8) teaches that God's experience of time differs from ours, so the apparent delay is not evidence of unfaithfulness. The delay serves God's merciful purpose: he is 'patient toward you, not wishing that any should perish' (v. 9). Augustine warns against calculating the date of Christ's return from the 'thousand years' formula.
context: Augustine's anti-chiliast eschatology — rejecting detailed timelines and calculations of the end — became the standard Western Catholic position. His emphasis on God's patience as the reason for the delay shaped pastoral preaching about eschatology for centuries.

author: Thomas Aquinas
interpretation: Aquinas reads the cosmic fire as a real physical event that will purify creation rather than annihilate it. The elements will be transformed, not destroyed — just as the human body is destroyed and reconstituted in resurrection. The new heavens and new earth will be materially continuous with the present creation but purged of corruption. Aquinas connects this to his broader metaphysics: matter is not inherently evil, so God's plan is to renew it, not replace it from nothing.
context: Aquinas's transformation reading — cosmic renewal rather than annihilation — became the dominant Catholic and much Protestant eschatology. It influences the current theological emphasis on new creation rather than escape from the material world.

author: John Calvin
interpretation: Calvin emphasizes the pastoral and ethical purpose of the passage: knowledge that the world will end in fiery judgment should produce holy living (v. 11), not speculative timelines. He reads 'the Lord is not slow' (v. 9) as a rebuke to scoffers who mistake divine patience for divine absence. The delay is God's mercy — time granted for repentance. Calvin warns against both presuming on the delay (complacency) and anxiously calculating the timing (speculation).
context: Calvin's characteristic emphasis on practical holiness over eschatological speculation reflected his broader theological method. His refusal to engage in date-setting or detailed millennial schemes set the tone for mainstream Reformed eschatology.

author: Richard Bauckham
interpretation: Bauckham argues that 2 Peter 3 directly addresses the theological crisis of the delayed parousia in the late first century. The scoffers' argument (v. 4) — 'where is the promise of his coming?' — was a genuine intellectual challenge. The author responds with three arguments: God's sovereignty over time (v. 8), God's patience and desire that all repent (v. 9), and the certainty of the coming judgment despite its delay (v. 10). Bauckham reads the 'elements' (stoicheia) as the heavenly bodies (stars, planets), not the four philosophical elements, being dissolved in cosmic fire.
context: Bauckham's WBC commentary is the most influential modern critical treatment of 2 Peter. His careful attention to the Hellenistic Jewish background of the letter's cosmology and eschatology set a new standard for interpreting the text within its original intellectual environment.

author: Thomas Schreiner
interpretation: Schreiner defends the Petrine authorship of 2 Peter against the critical consensus and reads the passage as genuine apostolic eschatology. He emphasizes the tension between God's patience (v. 9, wanting all to repent) and God's sovereignty (the day is coming 'like a thief'). Schreiner reads 'not wishing that any should perish' within the Reformed framework: God's patience extends the opportunity for repentance to all the elect whom he intends to save. The cosmic dissolution is literal but leads to renewal, not permanent destruction.
context: Schreiner's defense of Petrine authorship and his Reformed reading of verse 9 ('any' as 'any of the elect') illustrate how theological convictions shape exegetical conclusions. His reading is contested by Arminian scholars who take 'any' as universally referential.
